Audio: So in the post kind of survey, we found that a lot of students like to learn about visualization. So that's what we're going to do today and also on Tuesday of next week. So today is going to be our lecture, visualization one, which basically what we're going to do is we're going to talk about why we need to do visualization, what kind of visualization do we need to use and act the actual code that will help us to do to make different visualization plots so that you can make your own plots in different cases and we learn some Python libraries and the relationship between those libraries as well. text:
Audio: We have spent a lot of time in exploratory data analysis. We've seen how we can manipulate the data, how can we do an imputation, how can we make sense of whether our data is in the right format or not. We've seen a lot of the string functions, right? So last lecture was a lot of the string functions and regular expressions. What we're going to do today is actually make a visualization of our data set after we do a little bit of data cleaning. So we'll make visualization so that we summarize the trends and then we basically try to understand whether our data is ready for the next phase of the data science lifecycle, life cycle which is going to be the prediction and inference that we're going to go into pretty quickly in a couple of weeks we're going to see a lot of modeling. in data 100. So the places that we will do visualization in a data science life cycle, as you can see, is in two places. I also kind of bolded the report decision and solutions. So either we make visualization in the EDA phase or we make visualization in the part that we'll make reports or we're trying to share our insights when stakeholders are outsiders or outsiders of the data science life cycle. Audio: You have also seen visualization before so it's not going to be entirely new to how we make visualization in different plots. It's just going to be a different programming library that you will learn and we're going to also talk a little bit about the science behind making visualization and how to make a very effective visualization in data 100. You've seen line plot in data 8, those coming from data 8, you've seen a scatter plot and also homework 1, you had also plotted the histograms and data tends to really like histograms. And we're going to talk a little bit about histograms and how we can applaud it using the tools that you have in data 100. So the goal of general visualization, kind of going back to the data style cycle that I showed you is to kind of provide a high level description of the data or a high level visual of the data to ourselves or to communicate transmit the viewers. text:
Audio: So to put it formally, we in data 100 call the specifically two goals for visualization. There's one goal that it will help you understand your data a little bit better. So this is going to be part of your exploratory data analysis. What you're basically doing here is that you try to make some visual of your data, some columns, to make sense of the data. And also, you're trying to make sure that the visuals that you're creating are very kind of flexible, lightweight, and then you can iteratively change it to different columns and quickly visualize the trends in your data. We don't want to spend too much time in here, but we also want to make sure that we have a visual sense of a summary of the data in one column or in a specific set of columns. And the goal to that we want to call out for is the communicating the results with the stakeholders. This is what we're going to spend a lot of time in the next lecture talking about how to effectively use visualization that are very descriptive. It has the entire kind of, it utilizes all the channels of information that you can have with the viewer. Make sure that the plot is labeled properly. It has a title. It has access labeled and whatnot. And what kind of visual should we use in order to effectively communicate the results of the model. So kind of thinking about these two goals, generally you can see that we're not going to be about making pretty plots, although we will make pretty plots. But the idea here is to figure out. What is the pre-thinking that we need to do in order to make an effective plot and what is the audience of the plot is it going to be yourself as part of the idea or is it going to be stakeholders so that you can make effective decision in terms of how much time do you want to spend it and how much how much care you should put into the details of the plot that you're creating. So in that sense, these two lectures are divided between the lecture one or today's lecture is just generally talking about different kinds of plots looking into different types of variables and then figuring out what is the appropriate type of plot that we can use for those visualizations. We will also try to learn a specific technique that you haven't seen before is called carnal density estimation in order to estimate trends in a distribution. And we're going to talk about what distribution is. But we're going to basically see how we can plot kind of a normalized way of histograms to make sense of the distribution of the trends in a data set. In the second half, which is on Tuesday's lecture, we're going to come back and we're going to talk about different transformations that you need to do in order to make sure that your data is actually ready for the modeling. So today we're going to see that, okay, I'm going to do the visualization and although Although my data is clean, it is not in the format that necessarily I want. So that's the part that we have a loop in the Explorer 3D analysis phase that we go back to the transformation and then we plot again up until the point that it takes the form that we exactly want before we move on to the modeling phase. And we're going to talk about the science behind visualization and a little bit information channels how to utilize it effectively again in the next lecture. audio: So to put it formally, we in data 100 call the specifically two goals for visualization. There's one goal that it will help you understand your data a little bit better. So this is going to be part of your exploratory data analysis. What you're basically doing here is that you try to make some visual of your data, some columns, to make sense of the data. And also, you're trying to make sure that the visuals that you're creating are very kind of flexible, lightweight, and then you can iteratively change it to different columns and quickly visualize the trends in your data. We don't want to spend too much time in here, but we also want to make sure that we have a visual sense of a summary of the data in one column or in a specific set of columns. And the goal to that we want to call out for is the communicating the results with the stakeholders. This is what we're going to spend a lot of time in the next lecture talking about how to effectively use visualization that are very descriptive. It has the entire kind of, it utilizes all the channels of information that you can have with the viewer. Make sure that the plot is labeled properly. It has a title. It has access labeled and whatnot. And what kind of visual should we use in order to effectively communicate the results of the model. So kind of thinking about these two goals, generally you can see that we're not going to be about making pretty plots, although we will make pretty plots. But the idea here is to figure out. What is the pre-thinking that we need to do in order to make an effective plot and what is the audience of the plot is it going to be yourself as part of the idea or is it going to be stakeholders so that you can make effective decision in terms of how much time do you want to spend it and how much how much care you should put into the details of the plot that you're creating. So in that sense, these two lectures are divided between the lecture one or today's lecture is just generally talking about different kinds of plots looking into different types of variables and then figuring out what is the appropriate type of plot that we can use for those visualizations. We will also try to learn a specific technique that you haven't seen before is called carnal density estimation in order to estimate trends in a distribution. And we're going to talk about what distribution is. But we're going to basically see how we can plot kind of a normalized way of histograms to make sense of the distribution of the trends in a data set. In the second half, which is on Tuesday's lecture, we're going to come back and we're going to talk about different transformations that you need to do in order to make sure that your data is actually ready for the modeling. So today we're going to see that, okay, I'm going to do the visualization and although Although my data is clean, it is not in the format that necessarily I want. So that's the part that we have a loop in the Explorer 3D analysis phase that we go back to the transformation and then we plot again up until the point that it takes the form that we exactly want before we move on to the modeling phase. And we're going to talk about the science behind visualization and a little bit information channels how to utilize it effectively again in the next lecture.

text:
Audio: What is a distribution? To put it simply distribution is the summary of your data. What kind of summary? This summary has two pieces. So before I define it as the summary has the set of unique values that a variable in your distribution can take. And then the frequency with which each one of them will come up. Right, so let me give you an example to make sense of what do I mean by that. Let's think about all faculty at Cal. Each faculty has a home department, right? So if you have a data set of faculty names and the department affiliations and whatnot, if you want to summarize this data set so that you make a distribution out of it, what you need to do is you need to make sure that you capture all the unique departments that exist in the data set, right? So that's where you are looking at the set of values in your distribution. And then what you're going to look at, additionally is that we need an additional piece of information which is what is the number of faculty that are basically housed within each one of those departments, right? So that's the frequency with which each one of those departments have came up in your, have come up in your, um, in the entire distribution into the summarized entire data set as a distribution. What we will also make sure that we understand is that because we're looking at the distribution of all possible values in the data set, when we're looking at the proportion of faculty that are affiliated with each one of those departments, the summation of those proportions should add up to be 100 percent, because we're capturing everyone, right? We also need to make sure that all departments are representative in our population, meaning that the probability or the proportion of faculty belonging to each individual department is gonna be. zero or greater than or equal to zero if you have a department that doesn't have a faculty for whatever reason that's going to be a missing value in your data set. The additional information is that we also need to make sure that if you're looking at all of the samples in our data set and we're looking at the allocation of each one of those samples into different different values in your set. you need to make sure that the sum of the total number of values that you have adds up to be equal to your entire data set size. So you're not missing an instance. So making sure that there is literally one-to-one correspondence between your data set and the summary of the distribution that you're creating. let's put it into practice. This is an example of a bar chart. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

text:
Audio: So we're going to start by bar plot. So bar plot is going to be mostly used when we're going to plot a qualitative variable, whether that be ordinal or nominal qualitative variable. And sometimes we might also use bar plot for quantitative discrete variables. And quantitative discrete because if the number of instances is as small, then we can technically think about those as small number of options of those discrete quantitative values to be also kind of categorized. And that's why we can also apply to that case. What you saw in the previous slide of call was a bar chart. So this was a bar chart, again, an pollinated variable. You can see that what we are encoding, thinking about information channels that you're receiving is that what we encoded was the lengths. So the lengths of each one of these bars is showing you the proportion of, or the percentage of individuals in each one of those categories. What you're also seeing is that we are not using the vids information in any way, right? So the vids does not encode any information. So you can think about, okay, I have a two-dimensional plot and I have color on top of that. So technically this is the basic level 4 information channels that I'm only sorry 3 information channels that I'm only using 1 information channel which is along the x-axis which is I'm using for the links of these bars I'm not using any information about the links of the or the width of these bars and I'm not using any information about the color so the color does not include any information so this is a very basic bar plot And what we're going to work with in terms of visualizing different trends is we're going to work with the World Bank data set. audio: So what we're going to see today and also on Tuesday is two main programming library that we'll be using for Plotting. You might have heard them, you might have seen us importing them in some of the notebooks. We're going to talk about MAT plotlib and seabourne. And the good thing about them is that each one of them follow a specific template for different plotting functions. It's just that the name of the function that plots different types of plots is going to be different, but the template for how we invoke them is going to be exactly the same, or mostly the same. The template for MAC plotlet is that it basically gets a sequence. What is a sequence? A sequence can be a list, it can be an array, it can be a series object, or what have you. And then it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the x-axis, it gets a sequence of items that needs to be plotted on the y-axis and it will go ahead them plot them and use the proper name of the plot that you want to plot. Pass the sequences, it will do its own magic. So the 10th foot for invoking any math plotlet function would be first to import math plotlet. So I say import math plotlet. I tried to remember this alias PLT that we're using for math bloodlip as a short form for plots. So you just remember that this is kind of the core programming that we're gonna be using. And then the way that I'm evoking it as I'll... directly say PLT dot whatever name of the plotting function I have right so this is the name of the plotting function then I go ahead and pass the sequence of values that I want again the sequence can be any sequence it can be a less it can be an array or a cambia series object. pandas native plotting:
[ ]: # pandas native plotting
Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb ['Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ]:
sns. countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

pandas native plotting:
[ ]: # pandas native plotting
Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb ['Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ]:
sns. countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

Note that we concluded our call to plt. bar with a semicolon ( ; ). This suppresses any unnecessary output other than the plot. If we do not include
a semicolon, the plot will still generate, however, we will see extraneous text as well:

pandas native plotting:
[ ]: # pandas native plotting
Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb ['Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ]:
sns. countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

[ ]: wb[ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016']
[ ]:
sns . countplot (data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016')
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[' Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ] :
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
.
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]: wb[' Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' ]
[ ] :
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
.
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
-
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb [ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016 ']
[ ]:
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

Equivalently, we could use the countplot method of the Seaborn library to create our bar plot.
[ ]:
# Seaborn countplot
-
Above, we said that bar plots should only be used to visualize the distribution of a qualitative (categorical) variable. Why is that? Consider what
happens when we try to use sns. countplot to visualize a quantitative variable, gross national income per capita.
[ ]:
wb [ 'Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016 ']
[ ]:
sns. countplot(data=wb, x='Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016' )
plt.title("GNI distribution for different countries");
What happened? A bar plot (either plt. bar or sns. countplot ) will create a separate bar for each unique value of a variable. With a continuous
variable, we may not have a finite number of possible values, which can lead to situations where we would need many, many bars to display each
unique value.
To visualize the distribution of a continuous variable, we use a different type of plot:
Histogram
Box plot
· Violin plot
Box Plots and Violin Plots

Audio: I can make sense of what data is being presented here. But if you take a look for all of those instances that are really overplotted on the x-axis, that's an example that we're calling it overplotting, because I can't really read each one of those positions for each one of those bars. And then the height of the bars, you're seeing that for most cases, it's equal to one, right? which means that that was a value that only appeared once. So that's not the right way of visualizing quantitative values. So this is one of the reasons that we say bar plot is really good for qualitative, qualitative variable distribution, but not necessarily for quantitative ones. But if I had just only a few different quantitative values that I could actually have nice bars, then I could probably use that. So that's kind of the caveat here that we will try to be a verus. text:
Audio: You call sns.boxblood or you call sns.violent plot and you will get the boxblood and violent plot. What you're seeing is that generally they communicate the same set of information. What we're seeing is that the boxblood is generally showing you the trend of the distribution of your data. It shows me that the middle 50% of our data is within that box. And then we're gonna talk about different elements that are visualized in the box plot and kind of make sense of what is visualized there. Violin plot is very similar, but it also has the additional channel that this widths of this violin part is also encoding some information. It is a density function on top of a box plot, so we will tell you that what is the probability or what's the proportion of the data points that are exactly at any given point on that Y axis that you're going up, right? Whereas the box plot basically tells you that There are some points here, but I'm not going to tell you how many of each do you have, right? But I'm just going to tell you that on aggregate, 50% of your data is within the box. So let's move on and talk a little bit about What is encoded in box, blood and violent blood? Audio: So essentially box, blood is plotting, quartiles. What is a quartile? Core distance for four. So quartile means that we have a data set. We're going to sort it and we're going to chunk it into quarters. Right? So when we chunk it into quarters, then what we have is that I'm looking at the distribution. And I'm going to say that if I look at the lower 25% of my data, or what we call 25th percentile, what is the point or what is the quartile that we're going to call a Q1 that encodes that value? That Q1 is the point that 25 percent or first quartile is the point that 25 percent of the data is on it or below it. median. So basically this here and this point is our Q2 or median, which basically says that I have 50% of the data on or below this exact point. So the distance between Q1 and Q2 is obviously a quarter, right? And the distance from all the weight from the minimum up until Q1 is 25% right or a quarter of your data set. And then similarly we also have upper profile or Q3 which is also 75% which basically says that 75% of the data is on or below this exact point. Which means that 25% is above it. Right? That's how you can think about chunking your data set. When you sort it, you chunk it into four different pieces and you're basically looking at the trend in your data. What's important when we are understanding these partials is that we want to figure out what is the spread of our data. What we're interested in other words is that we want to see what is the interval that carries the middle 50% of our data. We want to make sure that our data is centralized as much as possible so that the majority of our data or the 50% of the middle 50% of our data is very centered. meaning that the range between Q1 and Q3 is as small as possible. What do we call this rent? We call this rent interquartile rent, which is effectively Q3 minus Q1. So this interquartel range is showing you the spread of the data. It basically tells you that, okay, if interquartel range is 10 units, it will tell you that 50% of your data is within 10 units. So you will basically have some kind of a spike up distribution in that ten units of the values in your distribution. If your inter quartet range is equal to five. In order to fit 50% of the data into that range, you need to have a sharp risk bike. So that's basically showing you how centered your data is going to be. The larger this intercarter range is going to be, it's basically going to show you that your data is more spreaded out. The smaller this value is, it will show you that your data is more centered around, or between around I would say Q2 or median and so on and so forth. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat

In order to fit 50% of the data into that range, you need to have a sharp risk bike. So that's basically showing you how centered your data is going to be. The larger this intercarter range is going to be, it's basically going to show you that your data is more spreaded out. The smaller this value is, it will show you that your data is more centered around, or between around I would say Q2 or median and so on and so forth.

So that basically means that if you have a smaller box, your data is more centered. If you have a larger or wider box, that means that your data has a wider distribution, so your data points are more spreaded out in the sense. So what are those kind of viscures that are at the top or at the bottom? What we would say is that empirically, we calculated and we said that a reasonable range of the data that has the center between or center 50% between Q1 and Q3 can be spreaded out. 1.5 times inter-clot, 1.5 times inter-clot, RTL range below Q1 and 1.5 times interquart TL range above Q3. So let's take a look one more time. We would say that if this is my center of the data, this is very 50% of the data is. empirically using some kind of a notion of Gaussian distribution, you can basically say that if I want to be sure that my data is from the same distribution and I'm not including all the outliers, I can extend these legs and I can say that I can go for the duration of 1.5 times interquarty range. below the q1 and 1.5 times inter quartile range above q3 and that's a reasonable range. That reasonable range means that I'm sure with 99.97% that my data should be within this especially grunge between the upper viscayr and the lower viscayr. Anything below it and anything below the lower risk or anything above that per risk is going to be an outlier. That means that it's very unlikely that those data sets or data points are from the same distribution or the same trend that we'd seen. So I'm not going to go over why 1.5 times interquartar range, but I want if you're interested, go and take a look at this link. It's basically an empirical way of saying that how can we make sure that we are summarizing the distribution based on the assumption that the distribution is a Gaussian distribution and make sure that any data point that we see any data point that we sample from this distribution is going to be within the lower viscure and upper viscure with 99.7. percent probability. So that's a summary of the box plot. So that's why we need to visualize our quantitative values using a box plot because this basically summarizes the entire trend. I don't need to know individual values, but I just need to know the distribution of it. a violin plot. plots, box plot, and on top of that, it shows you the density function on the curves. text:
Audio: So moving on to histograms. So we said that for quantitative values we are looking at box blog, we're looking at violin plus and the other way of summarizing trends is histogram. I'm not going to go into the details of histogram beyond just reminding you that with the histogram the way that their histograms are calculated is that the y-axis is encoded in a way that it shows in a way that the area of each bar encodes the percentage of data points that you have in each one of those categories. So think about histogram as a way of us sorting the entire quantity, the list of quantitative values that we have, bin them, right? So you can basically see that there is a bin for anything between 0 and for example, 17,000 and so on and so forth. And total, we have five different bins. And then we are calculating or we're plotting these bars in a way that the area actually encodes the percentage. So for example, in this case, what we're seeing is that for this very first bin, the percentage of data points that we have in the first bin is equal to the area of that bin. So we're calculating which times height and this is going to give us 78%. So 78% of the data in the first bin and so on and so forth. The way that we encode the height is such that We arrive at the area being 78 percent. That's what we call density. Right? It's not exactly the probability, but it's a value that when you calculate the area of that bin, you get at the probability distribution for a single value or for a single range of values. There are different ways that we can plot histograms using map bloodlub using seaborne. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

audio: So moving on to histograms. So we said that for quantitative values we are looking at box blog, we're looking at violin plus and the other way of summarizing trends is histogram. I'm not going to go into the details of histogram beyond just reminding you that with the histogram the way that their histograms are calculated is that the y-axis is encoded in a way that it shows in a way that the area of each bar encodes the percentage of data points that you have in each one of those categories. So think about histogram as a way of us sorting the entire quantity, the list of quantitative values that we have, bin them, right? So you can basically see that there is a bin for anything between 0 and for example, 17,000 and so on and so forth. And total, we have five different bins. And then we are calculating or we're plotting these bars in a way that the area actually encodes the percentage. So for example, in this case, what we're seeing is that for this very first bin, the percentage of data points that we have in the first bin is equal to the area of that bin. So we're calculating which times height and this is going to give us 78%. So 78% of the data in the first bin and so on and so forth. The way that we encode the height is such that We arrive at the area being 78 percent. That's what we call density. Right? It's not exactly the probability, but it's a value that when you calculate the area of that bin, you get at the probability distribution for a single value or for a single range of values. There are different ways that we can plot histograms using map bloodlub using seaborne.

text:
Audio: So we could also have overlayed histograms and the same way that we had side by side box plots in order to encode two variables. If we have two variables that we want to encode, we could actually use the color or the hue information in order to do that. So what I plotted here is that I calculated whether a country is in the north-sand hemisphere or southern hemisphere and I basically said that okay I want to look at the GNI or the north-sand hemisphere countries versus the southern hemisphere countries and I want to look at the distribution. It plots one histogram with one color, it plots another histogram on top of that that's why we call it overlayed histogram with two different colors. You You can see that the color attribute is going to come from the hemisphere column that I created. So it basically says that, okay, I'm going to segment them based on hemisphere. I'm going to basically have the orange one for Northern part and then the blue one for the southern part. And additionally, what they will do is it will create this legend for us. Most of the time, if color is involved in any way, we might want to add legend so that be at that additional contact, contextual information about what's flooded in orange and what's flooded in blue. And that's called a legend. So, some more kind of information that you can gather from histograms. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

text:
Audio: These are the things that you've not seen in data 8. Looking at the histogram and the reason that we plot histogram is to figure out that the kind of the distribution of the quantitative variable that we have. So for example, here on the left, you're seeing that there is a long tail in the right side of the distribution. We call it a right tail distribution. So a right tail distribution is going to be probabil, probabilismatically because when we are going to do the modeling, they're going to be large values that are less represented in the data, but they still exist and they might bias your model in some way. So we need to fix all of these issues before getting into the modeling. In the other part, you see a long left tail, we call it a left-discute distribution. What we are really interested in is look at the trend, look at the histogram and make sure that our data is not left-discute or right-discute in a sense, and our data is kind of normally distributed so that we can work with it is slightly better. We're gonna go into the details of what kind of issues that they can create and how to fix these issues in the next lecture and also in the modeling lectures to come. The additional information that we can gather is mode. Mode is basically showing you whether the peaks in your distribution or whether the peaks in your histogram. What we are really interested in is look at the trend, look at the histogram and make sure that our data is not left-discute or right-discute in a sense, and our data is kind of normally distributed so that we can work with it is slightly better. We're gonna go into the details of what kind of issues that they can create and how to fix these issues in the next lecture and also in the modeling lectures to come. The additional information that we can gather is mode. Mode is basically showing you whether the peaks in your distribution or whether the peaks in your histogram.

text:
Audio: We can have one peak, we call it unimodal, we can have two peaks, we call it bi-modal, we can have more than one peak, but we don't go and say try-modal and whatnot, we would just call it multi-modal data sets. So this kind of mode information is going to be exactly what we might want to gather and we might want to summarize in our data. But we're going to see why that's important and why we need to summarize it a little bit more to make sure that we understand the mode of our distribution understanding whether it's unimodal or bymodal in future lectures and also in modeling lectures, particularly. text:
Audio: As, like, for example, I have two things on a table. I have two data points, right? And I want to summarize the trends in my data point, or I have like multiple different bars. And I want to summarize it, but I don't want to show the entire data set or data points that I have to the user so that they make sense of what's happening. What I can do is I can put a fabric on top of all of these data points and then show them the trend or the shape of that fabric. So depending on the thickness of that fabric, they might even only see the tallest one or they might see if the fabric is a little bit lighter in texture. They might also to see some of the details of the other peaks or other data points that I have in my trend. So I want you to think about kernel density estimation as a way that we're trying to... Put kind of a fabric on top of our bars in a histogram and make sense of how can we normalize or how can we give a general visual about what's happening in the trend of our dataset. Why is it important in the first place? So let me show you why it's important. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

Python 3 (ipykernel)
O
Seaborn allows us to easily create side-by-side plots by specify both an x and y column.
[ ]:
sns. boxplot(data=wb, x="Continent", y='Gross domestic product: % growth : 2016' )
plt. title("The distribution of gross domestic product for different continents");
Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:
# The edgecolor' argument controls the color of the bin edges
gni = wb["Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016"]
plt.hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white")
# Add Labels
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
[ ] :
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016", stat="density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
We can overlay histograms (or density curves) to compare distributions across qualitative categories.
The hue parameter of sns. histplot specifies the column that should be used to determine the color of each category. hue can be used in many
Seaborn plotting functions.
Notice that the resulting plot includes a legend describing which color corresponds to each hemisphere - a legend should always be included if color
is used to encode information in a visualization!

Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:

Seaborn plotting functions.

Each bin of a histogram is scaled such that its area is equal to the percentage of all datapoints that it contains.
[ ]:
densities, bins, _ = plt. hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white", bins=5)
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt. title("A histogram of the distribution of GNI per capita");
print(f"First bin has width {bins [1]-bins[0]} and height {densities[0]}")
print (f"This corresponds to {bins [1]-bins [0]} * {densities [0]} = {(bins[1]-bins[0]) *densities[0]*100}% of the data")
In Data 100, we describe a "mode" of a histogram as a peak in the distribution. Often, however, it is difficult to determine what counts as its own
"peak." For example, the number of peaks in the distribution of HIV rates across different countries varies depending on the number of histogram bins
we plot.
[ ]:
# Rename the very Long column name for convenience
wb = wb. rename(columns={'Antiretroviral therapy coverage: % of people living with HIV: 2015' : "HIV rate"})
# With 5 bins, it seems that there is only one peak
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=5)
plt.title("5 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# With 10 bins, there seem to be two peaks
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=10)
plt.title("10 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# And with 20 bins, it becomes hard to say what counts as a "peak"!
sns.histplot(data=wb, x ="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=20)
plt.title("20 histogram bins");

Seaborn allows us to easily create side-by-side plots by specify both an x and y column.
[ ]:
sns. boxplot(data=wb, x="Continent", y='Gross domestic product: % growth : 2016' )
plt. title("The distribution of gross domestic product for different continents");
Histograms
You are likely familiar with histograms from Data 8. A histogram collects continuous data into bins, then plots this binned data. Each bin reflects the
density of datapoints with values that lie between the left and right ends of the bin.
[ ]:
# The edgecolor' argument controls the color of the bin edges
gni = wb["Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016"]
plt.hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white")
# Add Labels
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
[ ] :
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="Gross national income per capita, Atlas method: $: 2016", stat="density")
plt.title("Distribution of gross national income per capita");
We can overlay histograms (or density curves) to compare distributions across qualitative categories.
The hue parameter of sns. histplot specifies the column that should be used to determine the color of each category. hue can be used in many
Seaborn plotting functions.
Notice that the resulting plot includes a legend describing which color corresponds to each hemisphere - a legend should always be included if color
is used to encode information in a visualization!

Each bin of a histogram is scaled such that its area is equal to the percentage of all datapoints that it contains.
[ ]:
densities, bins, _ = plt. hist(gni, density=True, edgecolor="white", bins=5)
plt.xlabel("Gross national income per capita")
plt.ylabel("Density")
plt. title("A histogram of the distribution of GNI per capita");
print(f"First bin has width {bins [1]-bins[0]} and height {densities[0]}")
print (f"This corresponds to {bins [1]-bins [0]} * {densities [0]} = {(bins[1]-bins[0]) *densities[0]*100}% of the data")
In Data 100, we describe a "mode" of a histogram as a peak in the distribution. Often, however, it is difficult to determine what counts as its own
"peak." For example, the number of peaks in the distribution of HIV rates across different countries varies depending on the number of histogram bins
we plot.
[ ]:
# Rename the very Long column name for convenience
wb = wb. rename(columns={'Antiretroviral therapy coverage: % of people living with HIV: 2015' : "HIV rate"})
# With 5 bins, it seems that there is only one peak
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=5)
plt.title("5 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# With 10 bins, there seem to be two peaks
sns.histplot(data=wb, x="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=10)
plt.title("10 histogram bins");
[ ]:
# And with 20 bins, it becomes hard to say what counts as a "peak"!
sns.histplot(data=wb, x ="HIV rate", stat="density", bins=20)
plt.title("20 histogram bins");

Audio: If I go ahead and show you, for example, what happens if I have 10 bins in my distribution. I would say that, okay, I want you to plot HIV rate in the WB data frame, the stat being density, the density is plotted here on the ViAxis. And I want you to specifically use 10 bins. Don't infer how many bins I want. Use exactly 10 bins. You might want to arrive or you might arrive at the decision that your distribution is by model. Versus if I go ahead and say that, okay, the number of bins is equal to 20, then your decision might be very different. You might want to say that your distribution is multi-modal. So because the decision that we're making about the mode of the distribution and sometimes also about the skewness of the distribution is going to be controlled by this kind of arbitrary variable, we might want to go ahead and say that I'm going to smooth this entire distribution and I'm going to give you a kernel density estimation. And a kernel density estimation is again, It tends to defunction that is kind of similar to the histogram, but it's more smooth. audio: If I go ahead and show you, for example, what happens if I have 10 bins in my distribution. I would say that, okay, I want you to plot HIV rate in the WB data frame, the stat being density, the density is plotted here on the ViAxis. And I want you to specifically use 10 bins. Don't infer how many bins I want. Use exactly 10 bins. You might want to arrive or you might arrive at the decision that your distribution is by model. Versus if I go ahead and say that, okay, the number of bins is equal to 20, then your decision might be very different. You might want to say that your distribution is multi-modal. So because the decision that we're making about the mode of the distribution and sometimes also about the skewness of the distribution is going to be controlled by this kind of arbitrary variable, we might want to go ahead and say that I'm going to smooth this entire distribution and I'm going to give you a kernel density estimation. And a kernel density estimation is again, It tends to defunction that is kind of similar to the histogram, but it's more smooth. And how do we do this smoothness or making our plots or our curve more smooth?

Audio: So that was a love of complicated, but let me tell you. How we build a probabilistic notion into visualizing a distribution. So the core part of the kernel density estimation or how we're going to make this estimation is based on a kernel function. What is a kernel function? The kernel function says that I'm the probability function. And this probability function represents that what is the likelihood that every time that you sample a data point you will basically exactly get the same data point or you will get the data point that is slightly higher in value or is slightly lower in value. Say for example, I have the hiv rate sample one data point with the value equal to 20. In order for me to model the probability or the likelihood for me to be able to sample this data point again, I would say that there is a probabilistic word that is it is more likely for me to sample exactly the same data point. So there is a high probability here or a high density here when I'm going to basically say what's the probability that I'm going to get the same data point and as you increase the value or as you decrease the value. It is less likely less likely or less probable for you to sample those data points. That's how I'm going to build a probabilistic model. Right. So this basically says that based on all of your observations that you have, if you're assuming that your observations and your data set are your ground truths, and you're building a probability function on top of each individual data point that you have, you sum up or aggregate all of this information together, then you have a probability function that models the entire distribution. What you see in the kernel is how we can model the the probability or the likelihood of observing one sample or one row in your data frame or in your data set. But what we basically want to do is do exactly the same thing for all of the data points that we have in our data frame. So what you're seeing in this kernel is that this kernel is a probability function. All of the values starting from zero all the way to that 4D that I show they're all positive value. Right? So that's what we want from a probability. You can go ahead and calculate that the area under the care is equal to 1. Right? And it makes sense kind of for us to model this exact data point with HIV rate equal to 20 using this bill care function because it's more likely for you to get something that you think Think is your ground trust as you went out in the, in the world and you collected the data. And as you go to 21 to 22 or 30, then the probability of you observing those samples is going to drop, right? So that's basically what we're basically modeling with this kernel function. But exactly what we want is to model the entire data set, not only a single data point. So what we'll do is we'll basically say that let me put the kernel on top of all of the individual data points that I have, right? Because each one of them should be modeled with a probability function. And let me sum up all of those probability functions in order to build a probability distribution for the entire data set that I have. But the caveat that when you're looking at the entire probability distribution of your data set, you need to make sure that the area under the care over the entire distribution should be equal to one. As opposed to here, the probability the area under the care for this care and all for a single data point is equal to one. text:
Audio: So again, because I want to model each individual data point, but I can do is I can go ahead and say that that nice bell-carved shape, which is the Gaussian kernel, I'm going to put it on each individual data point that I have. So here is kind of a random toy example with five different data points that I created. We can plot something like this, which has indicators for different data points using something that's called rock blood. So we're going to see rock flood also more in the next lecture. But I have indicators for each one of those data points, 2.1, and so on and so forth. I want to turn this data set into something that I can understand from a probabilistic view. And I want to plot the probability distribution over this entire distribution that I have. text:
Audio: So that's the entire process. So without looking at the data, I give you five data points. This is going to be the trend that you're getting, which kind of makes sense. What you're expecting here is that you have two peaks here and then this data point contributes more to these first two samples and these two samples are kind of more cluster together. So that's where one mode comes here and another higher mode comes before it because it gets the contribution from the third data point in the middle. So kind of making sense of thinking about I had those five with six and now I'm trying to put a fabric on top of them and that fabric is going to tell me that okay you have a peak here or two points that are stacked on top of each other frequency around two and then, or three points. text:
Audio: You can plot this kernel density estimate plot using KDE plot. So that's the syntax. That was the math behind that. How do we arrive at it? And this is the kind of the plotting function that we have. So we call C born KDE plot. We say that, okay, my data set or my data frame is called points. Here I'm saying that my bandwidth is equal to 0.65. This is like really arbitrary value that I just chose in order for this plot to really look good. Here, I'm also saying that I want hot to be equal to tree. Hot to be cool to tree is kind of a nice thing, which will tell you that, okay, go ahead and extend the tails of this distribution, tree standard deviation from the minimum and from the maximum. So that's why you're seeing all of these hearts right here. This is because we're not cutting it at the exact location of the pins, but we're just letting it to smoothly drop onto zero and tree standard deviation. from the maximum or from the minimum of these parts. text:
Audio: So math behind the mechanics behind how we can create it. There's also another function that can plot histograms, that can also give us the KDE functions. And that's called this plot. This plot is what it gets is it will get something that's called kind. Which is similar to the native panda plotting. Remember that I would do plot kind equal to bar. This is kind of the same thing. This plot is a wrapper on top of the KDE plot his plots on and so forth so that you don't need to remember which one is which. So you just directly call this plot, meaning that I want to plot the distribution. But what do I want to plot about the distribution? I want to plot using the WB data set. I want to plot GNI data. I want to call that the type to be or the kind to be histogram. And I want the stat that you're showing on the y-axis to be density. It will create histogram for you. You could say that, okay, I'm doing this plot, but here I'm saying that the kind is equal to KDE. You don't need to specify that this stat is density because by default this stat is density because it is a density function at the end when you're creating the KDE plot. There's also this last thing that is called ECDF plot, which is empirical cumulative distribution function, which basically tells you like the cumulative distribution function that you want to create that like what is the percentage of data points that are below each individual data point. So you can go crazy instead of having the distribution plot, you can have the ECDF plot as well. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

text:
Audio: So on that aside, let's go back to the steps that we took in order to create the KDE plot. Remember that we had three different steps. So I'm going to put that in one equation and show you what those three steps were. It sounds a little bit complicated, but it is not. So the first step for us was to put the kernel on top of each one of those locations, right? When I say putting a kernel on top of each one of those locations, that means that we would say that I want a function that is on the x axis, it can take different x values. But it is centered around x i, it is centered around each one of the sample data points that you have because we're putting each one of these kernels on top of each individual data point. Okay. Alpha x and x i basically is the kernel function alpha is the bandwidth of that kernel function x is a value that it can take it can be any value on the x axis x i is various the center of that curve so you're basically thinking about putting the curve on each one of the data points. Then what we did was effectively a scaling it down. Remember, this was a scaling it down by a factor of 1 over n if you have n data points. And then the other factor here is that you're going to sum up over all of the possible data points that you have. If you have n different data points, you need to put a kernel on top of each individual one of them. That's where your i comes from. That's why we're, that's where your x i comes from. So put a kernel on top of each one of them, scale it down, sum it up, and at the end you have a function. And what is this function? This is a function of x. X can take any values. And then this function is parametrized by alpha. depending on the choice of alpha, then the type of the care and elensity estimate, the KDE function that you're going to get at the end is going to be different. text:
Audio: So quickly going over different types of kernels. We have the Gaussian kernel. The equation for a Gaussian kernel is not that important. You might have seen it before. The Gaussian kernel is that bell curve shape kind of function that you have that is centered around X i. So you would see that the maximum value that this curve can take is at the point that x is equal to x i. So that that numerator is equal to zero. So that e to the power of zero is equal to one. That's where we have the maximum value. And then as x is going to deviate from x i on both sides, then the probability is going to drop. And if you calculate this summation for of this function over all possible values, you will see that the area under the curve is equal to 1. And obviously, you know that this function is always positive, so it is a probability function that we can work with. So these are some details that center the wrong x i and Alpha controls the bandwidth. So here you're seeing that when I say that the kernel function is parametrized by alpha and I keep using alpha or bandwidth, it's because alpha appears here and alpha appears here, and alpha controls that bandwidth. So it will basically tell you that as you're moving away, if you're looking at that exponent thing at the top here, it will tell you that as you're moving away from Xi, how sharp should you drop the probability. Right, when alpha is a large value, you should drop the probability sharply because the denominator is large. Right, so that's how you can make sense of why alpha controls the bandwidth and because you're dropping sharply, it's going to be making a very kind of makes a very kind of centered peak. text:
Audio: The effect of band myths we can see it here. We could say that, okay, if I have a distribution and my distribution, if I look at it from the histogram perspective is like this, and I use different band myths, you're seeing that with alpha equal to point point 1, it is going to be very spiky, because I'm putting a spiky stuff on top of the data points and I'm averaging all of them. So those spikes are not going to merge together and form a nice kind of curvey shape. If I increase alpha to equal to one, I will start losing some of those noise that I saw in the previous case, but there is still some noise exists in my data. If I increase alpha to be equal to two, you're seeing that my curve is getting smoother and it's smoother in this case as well. So the choice of alpha is really your choice, but you just know that based on the alpha, you're changing, remember that fabric thing, you're changing the density of that fabric. So if your alpha is going to be a very high alpha, you're putting a very thick fabric on top of your distribution. So only the single peak is going to be captured. If you keep like lightening or choosing a fabric that's a little bit lighter, you will get a sense of all of those smaller peaks or smaller trends. So that's kind of a choice when you're making in order to make sure that do you understand enough about your distribution, do you understand what is the mood of your distribution and the skewness of the distribution and so on and so forth. There are other types of kernels such as box car kernel. Video: 1: Lec 07 - DS100 Fa24- Visualizat X

text:
Audio: It's not going to be used in practice, but you would basically see that it's still parametrized by alpha. It still has the center on x i's and the shape is going to be something like this. Area under the box is always equal to one. It's center that x i, for example, in this case, it's equal to four. for and then it takes in a value for the height or for the density so that the area under the curve is equal to one. And if we apply boxcar instead of the Gaussian kernel for the kernel density estimation, this is the kernel density estimation that we're going to see with a different kernel. But in practice most of the time we'll use Gaussian kernel. So we'll always see all of those like curvy shapes or nice curves that you're seeing that you are in the demo today.
